coredata.dc:title: Bio-nano emulsion fuel based on graphene quantum dot nanoparticles for reducing energy consumption and pollutants emission
coredata.dc:creator: Ettefaghi, Ehsanollah
Rashidi, Alimorad
Ghobadian, Barat
Najafi, G.
Ghasemy, Ebrahim
Khoshtaghaza, Mohammad Hadi
Delavarizadeh, Saman
Mazlan, Mohamed
coredata.dc:description: In this research, for the first time a mixture of diesel-biodiesel-water-bionanoparticles was prepared to simultaneously optimize the fuel consumption, reduce pollutant emission, and enhance the performance of diesel engines. To this end, graphene quantum dots as biodegradable nanoparticles were synthesized through the hydrothermal method. In this respective, the synthesized quantum dot nanoparticles were added at the concentrations of 20, 60, and 100 ppm along with 1, 5, and 10 vol% distilled water to the B15 fuel (the diesel fuel containing 15 vol% biodiesel). The results obtained from the engine experiments exhibited that when water and quantum dots were added simultaneously to the B15 fuel, the engine power was increased and the brake specific fuel consumption was reduced. In addition, the emissions of pollutants including HC, NOx and soot were decreased. However, the soot emission was slightly increased by adding the nanoparticles. For the sample containing 5% water and 60 ppm graphene quantum dot nanoparticles, the engine power was improved by 13.6% and the brake specific fuel consumption was reduced by 7.8% compared to those of the B15 pure fuel. Furthermore, emissions of pollutants including NOx, HC, and soot were decreased by 3.8, 29.2, and 33.7%, respectively, compared with the B15 pure fuel.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Diesel engine
Exhaust emissions
Biodegradable nanoparticles
Graphene quantum dots
Emulsion fuel
Biodiesel

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and functionalization of graphene quantum dots
Characterization of graphene quantum dot nanoparticles
Synthesis and characterization of carbon quantum dots
Power and specific fuel consumption of the diesel engine
Investigating the NOx emission from the diesel engine
Investigating the emission of HC pollutant from the diesel engine
Investigating soot emission from the diesel engine

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the Tarbiat Modares University and especially the Research Institute of Petroleum Industry (RIPI) of Iran for providing equipments to doing researches.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Engine and dynamometer set-up.
Chemical structure of quantum dots.
X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of GQD nanoparticles.
Infra-red spectra of GQD nanoparticles. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(a) TEM image and (b) photoluminescence spectra of GQDs.
X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of FGQD nanoparticles.
Infra-red spectra of FGQD nanoparticles.
(a) TEM image and (b) particle size distribution of FGQDs.
Photoluminescence spectra of FGQD nanoparticles.
Emulsion fuels of diesel-biodiesel-water containing 1, 5, and 10 vol% water.
Thermal conductivity coefficient of various prepared fuels.
Brake specific fuel consumption for emulsion nano-fuels containing GQD nanoparticles.
Brake specific fuel consumption for emulsion nano-fuels containing FGQD nanoparticles.
Emission of soot by using emulsion nano-fuels containing GQD nanoparticles.
Emission of soot by using emulsion nano-fuels containing FGQD nanoparticles.
The specification of chemical compounds used in this research.

Composition of different samples of emulsion fuels.

Comparison of means of main effects on BSFC (g/kWh).
Comparison of means of main effects on NOx pollutant (ppm).
Comparison of means of main effects on HC pollutant (ppm).
Comparison of means of main effects on the soot pollutant (1/m).

head.ce:title: Bio-nano emulsion fuel based on graphene quantum dot nanoparticles for reducing energy consumption and pollutants emission

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Material Research Cluster, Faculty of Bio Engineering and Technology, University Malaysia Kelantan, Jeli Campus, Jeli Kelantan, Malaysia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this research, for the first time a mixture of diesel-biodiesel-water-bionanoparticles was prepared to simultaneously optimize the fuel consumption, reduce pollutant emission, and enhance the performance of diesel engines. To this end, graphene quantum dots as biodegradable nanoparticles were synthesized through the hydrothermal method. In this respective, the synthesized quantum dot nanoparticles were added at the concentrations of 20, 60, and 100 ppm along with 1, 5, and 10 vol% distilled water to the B15 fuel (the diesel fuel containing 15 vol% biodiesel). The results obtained from the engine experiments exhibited that when water and quantum dots were added simultaneously to the B15 fuel, the engine power was increased and the brake specific fuel consumption was reduced. In addition, the emissions of pollutants including HC, NOx and soot were decreased. However, the soot emission was slightly increased by adding the nanoparticles. For the sample containing 5% water and 60 ppm graphene quantum dot nanoparticles, the engine power was improved by 13.6% and the brake specific fuel consumption was reduced by 7.8% compared to those of the B15 pure fuel. Furthermore, emissions of pollutants including NOx, HC, and soot were decreased by 3.8, 29.2, and 33.7%, respectively, compared with the B15 pure fuel.

ce:section.ce:para: Emissions of pollutants including carbon monoxide (CO), nitrogen oxides (NOx), unburned hydrocarbons (HC), sulfur oxides (SOx) and soot are the main problem of diesel engines [1]. Various approaches have been followed to enhance the performance and decrease the emission of pollutants from diesel engines which could also provide good results [2]. The EGR system [3], turbocharge [4] and supercharge [5] systems are among the practical methods employed to reduce the problems associated with diesel engines. However, the enhancement of the fuel quality and provision of conditions for the complete fuel combustion in the engine is of a higher priority since, in this method, there is no need for changes in the engine design and structure. Due to the economical benefits of diesel fuels, extensive researches have been devoted to enhancing the fuel quality and the complete combustion [6]. The use of bio-fuels, water, nanoparticles, and other additives is among the approaches that have been exploited to enhance the fuel and combustion quality. For instance, Hoseini et al. [7] studied the effect of biodiesel fuel on the performance and emissions of diesel engines. Moreover, zinc oxide nanoparticles and ethanox were used as additives for improving the diesel engine performance by Ashok et al. [8]. In another study, reduction of CO and NOx emissions of diesel engines was done by utilizing water and nanoparticles simultaneously [9].

In recent years, the application of different metallic and metal oxide nanoparticles such as Cu, Ag, Au, Zn, Al, Al2O3, TiO2, iron oxides, nickel oxide, silver oxide, zinc oxide, and cerium oxide as additives in diesel fuels has been investigated that also had favorable results [10]. However, serious concerns regarding the emission of toxic metallic and metal oxide nanoparticles to the environment still remain which leads to harmful problems [11]. In fact, metal and metal oxide nanoparticles can survive without any physicochemical changes in the environment for a long time which is indeed harmful [12]. Haristozov and Malsch [13] investigated the threat of nanoparticles to human health. Also, Joo and Zhao [14] studied the toxic effects of metal oxides on the environment.

Up to now, researchers have examined the effect of various nanoparticles on the performance and pollutant gas emission of diesel engines. Mirzajanzadeh et al. [15] used a hybrid cerium oxide-CNT catalyst for this purpose. In their research, a nanocatalyst was added to a mixture of diesel-biodiesel (B5 and B20) at concentrations of 30, 60, and 90 ppm and reported that when nanoparticles are used, a better combustion process is provided and emissions of CO, HC, NOx, and soot are reduced by 38.8, 71.4, 18.9, and 23.6%, respectively. Moreover, Zhang et al. [16] investigated the effect of CNTs and CeO2 nanoparticles on the performance and pollutants of a diesel engine exhaust. They added nanoparticles at the concentration of 40 ppm to the standard diesel fuel and conducted performance experiments using a heavy-duty four-stroke diesel engine. Results indicated that the emission of CO, HC and NOx were considerably decreased by 20%, 22.6% and 21%, respectively. Furthermore, the effect of NiO nanoparticles on the performance and pollutant emission of diesel engines was studied using a mixture of diesel-biodiesel by Srinidhi et al. [17]. They reported that for the nano-fuel containing 25% biodiesel, 75% diesel fuel, and 100 ppm NiO nanoparticles, the brake thermal efficiency was increased by 6.3% and the brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC) was decreased by 7.86% compared with the pure fuel. In addition, Hoseini et al. [18] added graphene oxide (GO) nanoparticles to a mixture of diesel-biodiesel and explored its effect on the performance and pollutant emission of the diesel engine. The base fuel contained 80% diesel, 20% biodiesel, and various concentrations of nanoparticles (30, 60, 90 ppm). In this research, it was revealed that by adding GO nanoparticles, the power and exhaust gas temperature were significantly increased. Additionally, significant reductions in pollutants of CO (∼5%–22%) and HC (∼17%e26%) were observed.

In another work, the effect of multiwalled carbon nanotubes and cerium oxide nanoparticles on the emission reduction of the diesel engine using biofuel was investigated by Abdul Sheriff et al. [19]; where, an ultrasonicator operating at a frequency of 24 kHz for a duration of 20 min was adopted to stabilize the nanoparticles inside the biofuel, by which the CO emission was decreased by 53.8% and the brake specific energy consumption was reduced by 16.4%.

In efforts to enhance the combustion process and reduce the emission of pollutants, the diesel-water emulsion has also been utilized [20]. In this respective, 2–6 wt% water was added to the biodiesel fuel by Zhang et al. [21] in which the 1:1 blend of Span 80 and Tween 80 was utilized in addition to the water. Their results showed that the highest reduction of the pollutants emission and highest performance parameters were achieved for the emulsion prepared by 4 wt% water. In addition, the impact of water on the performance of the fuel and pollutant emission from a diesel engine was studied by Vellaiyan [22]; water was added in two concentrations of 10 and 20 vol% and the performance was assessed by using a single cylinder, four-stroke diesel engine. It was shown that the water addition can improve the engine performance and reduce the emission of pollutants (NOx, HC, CO and smoke). Moreover, the best results with respect to the least BSFC and lower emission of pollutants were obtained for the sample prepared by using 10 vol% water. Furthermore, Seifi et al. [23] explored the influence of water on the engine power and torque, where, they added water at different concentrations of 2, 5, 8, and 10 vol% to the diesel fuel in which the sample containing 2 vol% water provided a higher power and torque compared with the base fuel; however, the engine power and torque were decreased with an increment of the water volume.

Various applications of nanotechnology have induced inevitable interactions between nanomaterials and the environment. Although nanotechnology has had positive effects on the performance of materials and systems, there are still concerns about the emission of metallic and metal oxide nanoparticles to the environment. As a new class of nanocarbons, carbon quantum dots were accidentally discovered by Zhou et al., in 2004 [24]. Carbon quantum dots are of great importance due to their low toxicity, environmentally friendly nature, low cost, and simple preparation methods. They can be synthesized using biocompatible materials such as ethanol, ascorbic acid, watermelon peel, orange juice, fruits peel, eggs, sugarcane, gelatin, and many other biocompatible materials [25]. For instance, Arumugham et al. [26] synthesized carbon quantum dots from Catharanthus roseus (white flowering plant) as precursor through the hydrothermal carbonization technique as a one-pot synthetic method. Also, Li et al. [27] utilized urea and citric acid to prepare carbon quantum dots. They placed the mixture of water, urea, and citric acid in a hydrothermal reactor for 6 h in order to perform the reactions. Moreover, Ye et al. [28] employed coal as the starting material to synthesize graphene quantum dots. They put the homogenous mixture of coal, sulfuric acid, and nitric acid at 120 °C in an oil bath under reflux for 24 h. The product was neutralized by the 3M NaOH solution through the filtration and dialysis membrane. In another research, Sahu et al. [29] followed a simple method to synthesize quantum dots using orange juice. In this method, 40 mL orange juice was placed at 120 °C under hydrothermal conditions for 150 min and 400 mg of quantum dot nanoparticles was obtained.

In the present research, to solve the problems arising from the use of metallic and metal oxide nanoparticles in the previous studies including toxicity, low stability, erosion and uneconomical; for the first time, the diesel-biodiesel-water-bionanoparticles compound was used. It was attempted to reduce the problems of the additives while benefiting from their advantages. So, bio-derived graphene quantum dots and water were used as additives to enhance the fuel consumption, reduce pollutant emissions, and upgrade the diesel engine performance. For this purpose, at first the graphene quantum dot nanoparticles were synthesized via a simple route. In the following, the prepared nanoparticles along with water were added to the B15 fuel to provide an enhanced performance. In order to assess the fuel performance, a single-cylinder engine was exploited to carry out the engine experiments.

As specified in Table 1 , the chemicals used in this research were purchased from Sigma Aldrich and Merck which were utilized without further purification. Three steps were followed to prepare the emulsion nano-fuel. Graphene quantum dots have a good stability in water. Therefore, at first distilled water was added to the diesel fuel followed by addition of biodiesel and nanoparticles to yield the final product in the second and third steps. Afterward, the pollutant emission and also the engine performance were evaluated via a single-cylinder, four-stroke engine. The Euro 4 diesel fuel as the base fluid for the preparation of the emulsion nano-fuel was purchased from a station in Tehran, Iran. Also, the biodiesel prepared from the waste of cooking oil was employed. Table 2 presents the properties of diesel fuel and biodiesel used in this research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and functionalization of graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: In the synthesis procedure, precursors of synthesizing the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) nanoparticles were urea and citric acid; where, 0.21 g citric acid and 0.18 g of urea were dissolved in 5 g deionized water. Then, the obtained solution was placed in an autoclave with a steel body and Teflon container which was then put in an electric furnace under the hydrothermal process at 160 °C for 4 h. After passing the time required for performing the reaction, the autoclave body was allowed to be cooled down to room temperature. In the next step, the resulting product was brought out of the autoclave and dried at 80 °C which was labeled as GQD.

After synthesizing the GQD nanoparticles, some approaches were used to further improve the surface area of the GQD nanoparticles and activate them. In this regard, initially the GQDs were carbonized under an inert gas atmosphere at 700 °C for 1 h. Afterward, a potassium hydroxide (KOH), as an activating agent, was adopted to provide a higher surface area and to activate the GQD nanoparticles; where, the KOH-to-nanoparticle ratio of 2:1 was considered under an inert gas atmosphere at 800 °C for 1 h. After activating by KOH, washing step of the resulting product was done by DI water for several times to reach the neutral pH followed by drying at 100 °C. Moreover, the nitric acid stream method was utilized for the surface modification and oxidation of carbon materials to form functional groups over carbon nanostructures and increase the dispersion. Finally, the product was functionalized and labeled as FGQD (functionalized graphene quantum dots).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of graphene quantum dot nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: A Philips PW 3710 device (30 KV, 35 mA, Cu-Kα) was utilized to record the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of GQDs and FGQDs. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Zeiss, EM900) was used to study the morphology of samples. Fourier-transform infrared spectra (FTIR, Perkin Elmer Spectrum-GX) were obtained over the wavelength range of 400–4000 cm−1. In addition, the photoluminescence spectrum of the quantum dots was recorded by a fluorescence spectrometer (VARIAN, CARY ECLIPSE). The dynamic light scattering technique (DLS) was employed to examine the size distribution of GQDs and FGQDs.

In the experiments, to evaluate the effect of water on the fuel, at first different concentrations of water were employed which are specified in Table 3 (i.e. 1, 5, and 10 vol%). In the next step, the emulsion fuel was mixed with 15 vol% biodiesel followed by addition of GQD nanoparticles (i.e. 20, 60, and 100 ppm). So, the stable emulsion nano-fuel containing GQD nanoparticles was prepared which is considered as a bio-nano emulsion fuel. Regarding the importance of stabilization of water throughout the prepared fuel, the fatty acid and its neutral salt were utilized as a lipophilic agent and the hydrophilic agent, respectively. For instance, in preparing 100 cc of 10 vol% diesel-water emulsion (diesel fuel which contained 10 vol% water), 7 cc oleic acid as fatty acid was used along with 0.7 cc monoethanolamine in a container equipped with a mixer at the rate of 500–1000 rpm. After passing a short while, a hydrophilic phase (fatty acid salt) was formed which neutralized part of the acid, and part of it remained in the reaction environment as the hydrophobic phase. Then, 81.3 cc of fuel diesel along with 10 cc of water and 1 cc of n-hexanol as the solvent assist were added to the previous solution which was stirred for a short time (i.e. about 1–5 min). Finally, water particles in the form of nanometer particles were completely dispersed inside the diesel fuel, the product of which was a transparent and stable diesel-water emulsion.
In order to evaluate the heat transfer capability of bio-nano emulsion fuel, thermal conductivity coefficient of the prepared samples were measured using KD2-Pro (Decagon devices, USA) at 25 °C.

To evaluate the concentration of above-mentioned emitting gases, the AVL DITEST GAS 1000 pollution analyzer was utilized which was made by AVL Company, Germany. This device is capable of detecting and assessing the concentration of different gases including NOx, HC, CO, CO2, and O2. The accuracy and detection range of these gases by the analyzer are tabulated in Table 5 . Also, the soot pollutant concentration was measured using DiSmoke 480 (AVL Company, Germany). The full-load state was considered for performing the experiments at 4 engine rates of 1800–2700 rpm. Moreover, all above mentioned experiments were performed at ambient temperature and pressure.

Results corresponding to the synthesis and characterization of graphene quantum dots and the production of emulsion nano-fuels are presented in this section. Moreover, results regarding the engine experiments are analyzed and explained in the following sections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 depicts the schematic view of GQD’s atomic configuration. Graphene quantum dots are generally composed of 1–20 wt% oxygenated functional groups which are in the form of carboxylic (COOH), hydroxyl (OH) groups, etc. In cases where the precursors contain a nitrogen atom, amine groups in the form of N–H, C–N, and N–H2 groups might be found in their structure.

As indicated in Section 2.1, GQD nanoparticles were synthesized by using urea and citric acid as starting materials. Fig. 3 illustrates the XRD pattern of the GQDs (i.e., GQD) which reflects their crystallographic structure. For the peak emerged at 2θ of 29.3°, the corresponding d-spacing between the graphene nano sheets is 0.3 nm which is almost equal to that of the (002) plane in graphite which confirms the formation of a carbon-based structure. The FTIR spectrum of the GQD nanoparticles is also demonstrated in Fig. 4 . Considering the fact that oxygen and nitrogen containing functional groups existed in the structure of the formed quantum dots, it was expected to observe bending and stretching vibrations between carbon and heteroatoms. According to Fig. 4, the broad adsorption peak in the range of 3100–3500 cm−1 reveals that OH groups corresponding to HO–CO and/or C–OH existed, while the N–H group overlapped with OH groups in this range. Furthermore, the adsorption peaks emerging at 1574, 1399, 1328, 11,710, and 767 cm−1 are related to CC, C–O, C–N, C–O, and C–H bonds in the prepared nanoparticles.

The TEM image and photoluminescence spectra of the GQD nanoparticles are given in Fig. 5 (a) and (b), respectively. As it can be clearly viewed, the GQD nanoparticles could be dispersed uniformly with the lateral size being less than 3 nm. Furthermore, a photoluminescence behavior was detected for these graphene quantum dot nanoparticles indicating that from these particles, a strong blue photoluminescence can be emitted under UV excitation. The wavelength of this emitted photoluminescence was about 365 nm which is another index confirming the successful synthesis of quantum dot nanoparticles.

To provide a higher specific surface area and also activate the surface of GQD nanoparticles, a specific heat treatment process was carried out on the as-synthesized GQD nanoparticles from which the FGQD nanoparticles were obtained. The XRD pattern of the carbonized, functionalized FGQD nanoparticles is presented in Fig. 6 . The peak at 2θ of 24° reflects the d-spacing of 0.37 nm which was increased compared with the GQD sample. The increase in the d-spacing of carbon structures can be attributed to functional groups existing over the particles. Also, the FTIR spectrum of the FGQD was investigated to determine its functional groups as illustrated in Fig. 7 . The broad adsorption peak in the range of 3200–3500 cm−1 similarly shows that there were OH groups of HO–CO and/or C–OH bonds. The peak at 1608 cm−1 is due to aromatic CC bonds which point to the carbon structure. Also, the adsorption peaks at 1716 and 1384 cm−1 correspond to C–O and CH3 bonds, respectively, and the peak at 1218 cm−1 is assigned to C–H and N–H bonds.

Fig. 8 represents the TEM image and DLS curve of the functionalized GQD nanoparticles (i.e. FGQD nanoparticles). It can be observed that FGQD nanoparticles have a dot morphology for which the mean size was approximately 1–3 nm. It is clear that, during the thermal treatment and functionalizing processes, the size of carbon quantum dot particles was not changed. Fig. 9 presents the photoluminescence spectrum related to the emission wavelength of FGQD nanoparticles under the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. It is evident that the emission wavelength was about 450 nm which is in the blue emitting range. The prepared graphene quantum dot nanoparticles are observed as dark-blue under the radiation of UV lamp by the wavelength of 365 nm.

As already mentioned, oleic acid was used as the lipophilic agent and its neutral salt was utilized as the hydrophilic agent to disperse and stabilize the water particles in the nanometric scale throughout the diesel fuel. In fact, in order to have a stable emulsion with uniform properties, the surface tension of hydrocarbon and water phases should be balanced. A crucial factor which can result in the preparation of a stable nano-emulsion is the balance between water and hydrocarbon phases. Fatty acids with a carbon atom chain of 12–20 and alkylphenols for which the alkyl group contains 8 or more carbon atoms are among the lipophilic or hydrophobic surface-reactive materials which can be effectively dissolved in hydrocarbon fuels. In addition, hydrophilic surface reactive materials are composed of alkylene polyamines and/or alkanolamines and have a hydrophobicity/hydrophilicity balance of 12.

Fig. 10 illustrates the emulsion nano-fuel samples containing different amounts of water beside the pure B15 fuel. It is clear that the prepared samples had good transparency. As there are many functional groups in quantum dots, they have good stability and dispersion in water. Consequently, in the third step, carbon quantum dot nanoparticles of GQDs and FGQDs were added at three concentrations of 20, 60, and 100 ppm to the diesel-biodiesel-water emulsion. After adding nanoparticles to the fuel, the container of the sample was placed in a bath ultrasonic for 5 min. To examine the stability of nano-fuels, the samples were poured in transparent glass containers and kept in steady condition for 6 months and visually analyzed. After 6 months, there was no change in the phases of the samples and no precipitation occurred.

To label the prepared fuel samples, the B15Wn-GQDm term was used in which B15 shows the based fuel (diesel fuel containing 15 vol% of biodiesel), water sign is “W” content of which is given by “n” in volume concentration, and “m” implies the nanoparticles content in ppm.

In this section the influences of different independent variables on the dependent variables are investigated by employing the novel diesel-biodiesel-water-nanoparticle emulsion fuel. To interpret the results, a factorial design in the form of a completely random design was used and repeated three times. The experiment data were interpreted by the analysis of variance (ANOVA) and the comparison of means on the results was performed using Tukey’s method by Minitab software. In the following, results corresponding to each variable are investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Power and specific fuel consumption of the diesel engine
ce:section.ce:para:
The acquired ANOVA results indicated that variables such as engine speed, nanoparticle type, nanoparticle concentration, and water volume fraction could affect the power and specific fuel consumption of the engine substantially, (i.e., in the range of 1%). The results obtained by varying each factor are given in Table 6 from which the distinct impacts of each factor can be compared. It should be mentioned that in Tables 6–10
which correspond to comparison of the average values, the letters of a, b and c specify whether the factors are considerable or not.

The results of comparing the average values showed that the engine power was slightly increased by adding water to the B15 fuel in 1 and 10 vol% compared with the B15 fuel. The average power was 4.7 kW for the pure B15 fuel. Nevertheless, the addition of 5 vol% water increased the engine power considerably. As a rule, water addition to the fuel can lead to two influences: decreasing the heating value and resulting in a phenomenon called micro-explosion. In spite of the fact that when heating value of fuel is decreased, the engine power is accordingly reduced, occurrence of the micro-explosion phenomenon can provide a condition through which the fuel and air are mixed better so that the combustion process is carried out more effectively. To interpret the micro-explosion process, when the emulsion is sprayed on a hot place such as the combustion chamber, heat is transferred to the emulsion droplet. When the temperature of water particles reached the super-hot conditions, the quick expansion of water bubbles causes the expansion and explosion of the whole emulsion droplet and ultra-fine diesel particles are formed. The micro-explosion phenomenon is actually the secondary atomizing of injector output due to the quick evaporation of water existing in the emulsion droplet through which air and fuel are mixed better. Therefore, based on the results, the micro-explosion phenomenon has overcome the reduction of heating value which, in turn, has increased the engine power for the sample containing 5 vol% water. Furthermore, some researchers have reported that when water exists in a fuel, the combustion delay is raised [21]. Bulent Koc and Abdullah have also reported an increase in the engine power and torque for the emulsion containing 5 vol% water [30]. Maawa et al. have also reported that adding 5 wt% water to the B20 fuel can increase the brake power of the diesel engine which is due to the increase in the ignition delay and inducing a higher pressure with the combustion chamber [31]. Moreover, according to study of Seifi et al., adding up to 5 wt% water to the diesel fuel leads to improving the engine power. They have attributed the improvement in the engine power to the micro explosion phenomenon which resulted in decreasing the size of fuel particles and a better mixing with air [23].

Regarding the effect of quantum dot nanoparticles on the engine power, results show that the addition of GQD nanoparticles to the fuel led to an increase in the engine power in comparison to the B15 pure fuel and biodiesel-water emulsions. However, FGQD nanoparticles had a negative effect on the engine power. The average power for the samples B15 and B15 containing GQD and FGQD nanoparticles was 4.7, 5.1 and 4.3 kW, respectively. In these experiments, the sample which was prepared by using 60 ppm nanoparticles possessed the best performance. An increase in the engine power due to the addition of GQD nanoparticles can be attributed to the better mixing of fuel and air through increasing the contact area and presence of oxygenated functional groups over the GQD nanoparticles which could compensate for the lack of oxygen in the combustion chamber. Therefore, a more complete combustion was performed which increased the engine power. Similar results have been reported in the literature. Mirzajanzadeh et al. [15] exploited 90 ppm MWCNTs-CeO2 nanoparticles as an additive in a mixture of diesel-biodiesel by which the power of the nano-fuel was improved by 7.8% compared to that of the base fuel.

The negative, decreasing effect of FGQD nanoparticles on the engine power can be due to the structure and the synthesis procedure of FGQD nanoparticles. In other words, as mentioned in Section 2.1, the carbonization process was performed to increase the specific surface area of FGQD nanoparticles. As the process was performed at a high temperature, part of the oxygenated functional groups was removed, and an increase in the surface area of FGQD nanoparticles led to a considerable increment of the heat transfer capability for the FGQD nanoparticles. An increase in the heat transfer in the combustion chamber reduced the thermal efficiency of the engine and, consequently, a lower power was provided. To thoroughly explore this phenomenon, the thermal conductivity coefficients of B15W5-GQD60 and B15W5-FGQD60 emulsion nanofuels and pure diesel and biodiesel were evaluated at 25 °C using the KD2 instrument. The results which are given in Fig. 11 exhibit that the heat transferring capability of the fuels which were prepared by adding water and quantum dot nanoparticles was improved significantly. As expected, the highest increase in the heat conduction coefficient was obtained for the fuel containing FGQD nanoparticles in which the thermal conductivity coefficient was increased by 15% compared with the B15 pure fuel. This is due to the increment of the surface area of FGQD nanoparticles after the carbonization and activation process. Finally, based on the results, the B15W5-GQD60 is determined as the best sample to enhance the engine power. The average amount of power for samples B15 and B15W5-GQD60 was 4.7 and 5.3 kW, respectively. So, the engine power using B15W5-GQD60 was increased by 13.6% compared with the B15 pure fuel.

In order to evaluate the BSFC value, the total fuel consumption was divided by engine power at a given rate. As a result, the BSFC value will be decreased by increasing the engine power at a specific rate. The results associated with each factor with respect to the BSFC value are given in Table 7. Also, the data related to the BSFC values of various emulsion nanofuels are shown in Figs. 12 and 13
.

As previously noted, the addition of GQDs to the fuel increased the engine power due to different factors. Therefore, the BSFC value of the nanofuel containing GQD nanoparticles was decreased compared with the B15 pure fuel which has been proved by results of engine experiments. However, the results obtained for nanofuels containing FGQD nanoparticles were different. In fact, as the FGQD nanoparticles led to a reduction in the thermal efficiency and engine power, the BSFC amount was increased as shown in Fig. 13. Also, the average amount of the BSFC for the B15 pure fuel was 360.3 g/kWh.

On the one hand, the micro-explosion phenomenon due to presence of water in the form of nanoparticles resulted in a better mixing of fuel and air and, as a consequence, a better combustion occurred which could provide a decrease in the BSFC. The effect of this phenomenon can be well understood from the samples containing 5 vol% water. According to the results, the average amount of BSFC of the B15W5-GQD60 sample was decreased by 7.8% compared with the B15 pure fuel. Of course, the lowest BSFC value belonged to the B15W5-GQD60 sample at 1800 rpm for which the BSFC value was decreased by 8.8% compared with the B15. The results of Ithnin et al. also demonstrated that adding 5 vol% water to the diesel fuel can decrease the brake specific fuel consumption by 3.89% [32]. Furthermore, based on results of Agbulut et al., adding metal oxide nanoparticles, such as Al2O3, TiO2, and SiO2, to the diesel fuel, can reduce the BSFC [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigating the NOx emission from the diesel engine
ce:section.ce:para: The only nitrogen oxide species which can be formed at the combustion temperature of the engine is the nitrogen monoxide (NO). This pollutant gas can be converted to nitrogen dioxide (NO2) while exiting the exhaust to the atmosphere. In general, the main factor leading to the formation of nitrogen oxides is the high temperature of combustion chambers because, at high temperatures, O2 molecules are dissociated to O atoms and, as a result, NO is formed. The NO formation rate is increased exponentially with the temperature of burned gases and also depends on the concentration of oxygen atoms in the combustion chamber. When the experiments were performed at higher combustion temperature and oxygen concentration, the emitting content of the NO was increased. Therefore, it can be stated that by decreasing the local temperature of the combustion chamber, the emission of nitrogen oxides can be limited.

In this research, results of ANOVA showed that factors including engine speed, nanoparticle type, nanoparticle concentration, and water volume fraction had considerable effects on the NOx emission in the range of 1%. Results of the comparison of means of the main effects on the NOx pollutant are presented in Table 8.

Based on the obtained results, the average amount of the NOx emission of the B15 pure fuel was about 172 ppm. Results of comparing the averages showed that the addition of water reduced the NOx emission such that, by increasing the amount of water to 10 vol%, the NOx emission was decreased considerably. This is originated from the high latent heat of water as it could absorb the heat of the cylinder and eventually decreased the combustion chamber temperature locally while evaporating the water particles. Moreover, in further researches almost the same results have been obtained with respect to the impact of emulsion diesel-water on the emission of NOx pollutant. Elsanusi et al. reported that adding water to the mixture of diesel-biodiesel can reduce the NOx emission and by increasing the water concentration up to 15 wt%, the NOx amount was decreased [34]. Moreover, Ithnin et al. revealed that adding 5 wt% to the diesel fuel decreased the NOx emission by 31% [32].

Considering the concentration of the emitting NOx, the results obtained for GQD and FGQD nanoparticles were different. According to Table 8, addition of GQD nanoparticles increased the NOx emission, as the presence of oxygenated and nitrogen functional groups over nanoparticles formed NOx in the combustion chamber. However, for FGQD nanoparticles, the NOx emission was decreased considerably. This is due to a local decrease in the temperature of the combustion chamber due to an increase in the heat transfer capability of nano fuels and also a lack of oxygenated and nitrogen functional groups which are in accordance with the effect of FGQD nanoparticles on the engine power. Altogether, it can be stated that the simultaneous addition of water and carbon quantum dot nanoparticles to the fuel led to a decrease in the NOx emission. For example, according to the results of the performed experiments, for the B15W5-GQD60 sample, the emission of NOx pollutant was reduced by almost 3.8% at various rates compared with the B15 pure fuel. In fact, the NOx emission of the fuel was decreased by 32.8% compared with the B15 pure fuel by using the B15W10-FGQD60. The average amount of NOx emission for the B15W5-GQD60 and B15W10-FGQD60 samples was 165.4 and 115.6 ppm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigating the emission of HC pollutant from the diesel engine
ce:section.ce:para: In general, unburned hydrocarbons are parts of fuel which have not participated in the combustion process and exit the engine unburned. The main reason contributing to this phenomenon is the incomplete combustion. Therefore, by providing conditions leading to a more complete combustion, a lower amount of unburned hydrocarbons will be emitted. In this research, results of ANOVA revealed that factors including engine speed, nanoparticle type, nanoparticle concentration, and water volume fraction can affect the HC emission considerably (i.e. in the range of 1%). Moreover, the effect of each variable on the final concentration of the HC is presented in Table 9.

The average amount of the HC emission for the B15 pure fuel was about 127.5 ppm. According to Table 9, it was also found that the addition of water and GQD nanoparticles to the fuel in optimum concentrations led to a better combustion process and reduced the HC emission. Based on the results, the average amount of the HC emission of the B15W5-GQD60 sample was 90.24 ppm and was decreased by 29.2% compared with the B15 pure fuel. In other words, the better mixing of fuel and air due to the micro-explosion phenomenon and the presence of oxygenated functional groups over GQD nanoparticles led to a more complete combustion process, thereby decreased the HC emission. The same results are reported in the literature; Kumar et al. [35] studied the emission of diesel engines powered by nano-emulsion. They have reported that the emission of pollutants including smoke, NOx, CO and HC were reduced by 24.2, 9.7, 18.4 and 16%, respectively, for the sample containing 10% water. Khalife et al. have shown that adding 4 wt% water to the B5 fuel results in decreasing the HC emission by 28% [36]. Additionally, Vellaiyan declared that addition of 10% water to the biodiesel fuel decreased the HC emisiion by 43.8% compared to the base fuel [22].

Nevertheless, different results were obtained for the sample containing FGQD nanoparticles. The HC emission was increased for the fuels containing FGQD quantum dots. As previously mentioned, this is due to the reduction of thermal efficiency of the engine as a result of an increase in the heat transfer capability of nanofuels which led to a decrease in the engine temperature. It is also believed that a lower amount of oxygenated functional groups over FGQD nanoparticles compared with GQD nanoparticles caused the increase in the HC emission. Based on the results, the highest and lowest HC emissions compared with the B15 pure fuel are related to the B15W10-FGQD60 and B15W5-GQD60 samples, respectively. Moreover, Mirzajanzadeh et al. have reported that exploiting hybrid Cerium oxide/CNT nanoparticles can decrease the HC emission by 71% [15].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigating soot emission from the diesel engine
ce:section.ce:para:
Soot is mainly formed due to the local lack of oxygen and the incomplete combustion in diesel engines and the best way to reduce the emission of soot is the better mixing of fuel and air and providing enough oxygen. In this research, results of ANOVA indicated that factors including the engine speed, nanoparticle type, nanoparticle concentration, and water volume fraction can have considerable effects on the soot emission in the range of 1%. Results of the comparison of mean values of the main effects on the soot pollutant are presented in Table 10. Also, results regarding the emission of soot for emulsion nanofuels containing GQD and FGQD nanoparticles are shown in Figs. 14 and 15
, respectively.

The consequences acquired by comparing the average values confirmed that by adding water to the fuel, a considerable change occurred and the soot emission was decreased significantly. These observations can be associated with the better combustion in the combustion chamber which can be attributed to the above-mentioned micro-explosion phenomenon and also oxidation of soot compounds. In this regard, the lowest soot emission was for the sample which was prepared by using 10 vol% water which has also been reported in the literature. Koc and Abdullah [30] reported that increasing the water concentration in the biodiesel nanoemulsion reduced the soot opacity up to 58.6%. Elsanusi et al. have also reported that 15 vol% water in the biodiesel fuel can decrease the soot emission by at least 20% [34].

By examining the effect of nanoparticles on the soot emission, it was found that, unexpectedly, the addition of carbon quantum dots increased the emission of soot from the engine exhaust. In other words, due to the better mixing of fuel and air which provides more oxygen in the combustion chamber of the engine, the emission of soot was expected to decrease. In fact, the addition of GQD and FGQD nanoparticles to the emulsion fuels led to an increase in the soot emission. Carbon quantum dot nanoparticles caused an increase in the opacity of gases exiting from the exhaust. Consequently, the Opacity Meter device showed higher values. In addition, by increasing the amount of nanoparticles, the emission of soot was increased which shows the direct effect of nanoparticles on the soot emission. Of course, the simultaneous addition of water and quantum dots led to a decrease in the soot emission for the samples containing GQD nanoparticles compared with the B15 pure fuel. However, the soot emission was slightly increased for the samples containing FGQD nanoparticles. Based on results, the average amount of the soot emission of B15W5-GQD60 sample was 6.7
1 m
and was decreased by 33.7% compared with the B15 pure fuel. The average amount of soot emission for the B15 pure fuel was about 10.1
1 m
. Also, among the different samples evaluated in this research, the lowest soot emission was about 3.26
1 m
which was related to the B15W10-GQD20 at 2700 rpm.

In this research, carbon-based nanomaterials were used to prepare an environmentally-friendly fuel. Mixtures of diesel-biodiesel-water-biocompatible nanoparticles were prepared to decrease the emission of pollutants and enhance the engine performance. Carbon quantum dot nanoparticles were prepared using citric acid and urea and were added to the B15 fuel at concentrations of 20, 60, and 100 ppm along with water. Results showed that the simultaneous addition of water and GQD quantum dot nanoparticles to the diesel fuel increased the engine power and reduced the BSFC. The best performance was obtained for the sample prepared by 5 vol% water and 60 ppm nanoparticles. For the B15W5-GQD60 sample, the engine power was increased by 13.6% and the BSFC value was decreased by 7.8% in comparison to the B15 pure fuel. Moreover, the emission of pollutant gases from the diesel engine exhaust was decreased by using the emulsion nanofuel. For the B15W5-GQD60 sample, compared with the B15 pure fuel, NOx, HC, and soot emission was decreased by 3.8, 29.2, and 33.7%, respectively. Indeed, the obtained results showed that the addition of graphene quantum dot nanoparticles to the fuel slightly increased the soot emission which was due to the increase in the opacity of gases exiting from exhaust.

Ehsanollah Ettefaghi: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Alimorad Rashidi: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Barat Ghobadian: Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Gholamhassan Najafi: Advisor, Validation, Writing - reviewing. Ebrahim Ghasemy: Data curation Writing - original draft, Mohammad Hadi Khoshtaghaza: Advisor, Validation, Writing - Reviewing Saman Delavarizadeh: Data curation, Mohamed Mazlan: Validation; Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Tarbiat Modares University and especially the Research Institute of Petroleum Industry (RIPI) of Iran for providing equipments to doing researches.
